title: Part I: The Founding Generation
paragraphs: —What is it?—What is it?—What does it mean?

Tony Buddenbrook, who was almost eight years old, sat in the window of her grandfather's great salon on Mengstrasse and was being catechized by her grandfather in the Heidelberg Catechism. But all those words, though she had been able to recite them quite correctly only the week before, had now fled her memory entirely.

Old Johann Buddenbrook regarded his granddaughter with a mixture of severity and amusement through his small pale-blue eyes. He was a tall man, still upright at seventy, with a rosy complexion, white hair powdered with flour in the old-fashioned style, and a large aquiline nose. He sat comfortably in his armchair near the window, one leg thrown over the other, slowly turning the pages of the catechism in his enormous hands.

It was a glorious afternoon in the first days of October in the year 1835. The sun was already low; it lay in broad stripes of golden light across the large old salon with its faded wallpaper and its massive furniture covered in faded green damask. A broad beam of sunshine fell across the round table in the middle of the room and lay on the parquet floor, where it seemed to wax and wane, now brighter, now dimmer, as the window curtains swayed gently in a little wind.

The Buddenbrook family occupied the whole second floor of the house in Mengstrasse; the first floor was given over to offices and warehouses of the firm of Johann Buddenbrook, Merchant. It was the same firm which old Johann had inherited from his father, who had founded it in 1768.

Elisabeth Buddenbrook, the grandmother, sat in a corner of the sofa, half-asleep, a piece of needlework in her hands. She was a stout, comfortable woman who had long ago ceased to understand why anyone needed to talk or think at all, when there was so much good food in the world and the firm was doing well.
'So, Tony,' said old Johann, who was not a man to let a child escape with vague answers, 'let us try again. What does it mean?'
Tony screwed up her face and tried to look studious.
'It means,' she said carefully, 'it means—that we are to love God with all our hearts and—'
Her grandfather waved his hand impatiently.
'That is what it says. I ask what it means.' He leaned forward, putting the book on his knee. 'Listen, child. We say: This is most certainly true. But what does that mean?'
Tony looked at the sunbeam on the floor.
'It means,' she said, with sudden inspiration, 'that it is really and truly true.'
Old Johann burst into laughter—a strong, clear, hearty laugh that shook his whole body.
'That is most certainly true!' he repeated, between his laughter. 'Capital! Capital! Elisabeth, did you hear that?'
But Grandmamma had fallen quite asleep.

title: Part II: Tony's Marriage
paragraphs: Tony Buddenbrook was twenty years old in the summer of 1845, and she was the pride of the Buddenbrook family. She was tall and fair, with the family's characteristic nose—slightly arched—and clear blue eyes and a way of holding herself that said unmistakably: I am a Buddenbrook. Not arrogant—the Buddenbrooks were not arrogant—but secure. She knew who she was.

The firm was doing well. Her father, the new Johann Buddenbrook—Consul Buddenbrook, they called him now—had expanded the business, and the house in Mengstrasse was full of life and prosperity. Tony was educated and accomplished; she played the piano and spoke French and had opinions about literature.

In the summer of that year she fell in love for the first time. His name was Morton Schwarzkopf—the son of the harbor pilot in Travemünde, where Tony was spending her holiday. He was a medical student, earnest and idealistic, with no money and no name. She liked him very much.
But when she came home and told her father, the Consul was not pleased.
'My dear child,' he said, 'you know I think the world of you. And I want you to be happy. But a harbor pilot's son—a medical student without means—'
'He is a fine person,' Tony said.
'I have no doubt of it,' her father said gently. 'But we have a duty, Tony. Not just to ourselves. The firm—the family name—these things ask something of us.'
Tony understood. She had always understood. The Buddenbrook sense of duty was bred into her. She wrote Morton a letter and gave up the summer's happiness.

Instead she married Grünlich—Benjamin Grünlich, a Hamburg merchant with blond side-whiskers and an excellent credit rating. Her father had arranged it, and the firm had approved it, and the family had approved it, and Tony had said yes, because she was a Buddenbrook and Buddenbrooks put the family first.
She did not love Grünlich. She tried. He was not a bad man—merely fussy and vain and rather dull. But she tried.

The marriage lasted four years. Then Grünlich went bankrupt—a real bankruptcy, complete and humiliating—and it came out that he had married Tony specifically because he had hoped to get money from the Buddenbrook firm to cover his debts. The old Consul came and fetched his daughter home, and Tony came home with a small daughter of her own, Erika, and a heart that had grown harder and quieter.
She was twenty-four. She had been married and divorced. She was a Buddenbrook.
'Never mind,' said her father. 'You are home now.'
'Yes,' said Tony. 'I am home.'
She set her trunk down in her old room and looked out the window at Mengstrasse, and the firm's sign over the warehouse door, and the familiar sky above the familiar rooftops of Lübeck.
She was home.

title: Part III: Thomas Buddenbrook
paragraphs: Thomas Buddenbrook became head of the firm in 1855, when his father the Consul died. He was thirty years old, and he was, everyone agreed, the best Buddenbrook there had ever been—more capable than his grandfather, more polished than his father, with a head for business and a gift for social relations that made him the most admired man in Lübeck.

He had married well—Gerda Lebrecht, a beautiful woman from Amsterdam with red-gold hair and an extraordinary talent for the violin. She was a little strange, people said; she cared more for music than for society, and her eyes were sometimes very far away. But she was a great lady, and the house on Breite Strasse, where Thomas and Gerda lived, was the finest house in the city.

Thomas worked very hard. He sat in his office from morning to evening and supervised every aspect of the firm's business. He cultivated the right connections, gave the right dinners, said the right things at the right moments. He was perfect—so perfect that sometimes, in the evening, sitting alone in his study while Gerda played the violin in the next room, he felt that perfection had swallowed him up entirely.
What was he, underneath? He was not sure. He was Senator Buddenbrook, head of the firm, husband of Gerda, father of young Hanno. But what was Thomas—Thomas himself?

He found a book, in these years of doubt, that spoke to him strangely. It was by a philosopher named Schopenhauer. He read it late at night, and what the philosopher said disturbed him and comforted him at the same time. That the individual was merely a wave on the ocean of the will—that death was not an ending but a return—that in dying we gave back what was never truly ours—
He put the book away. He had too much to do to think such thoughts.

His son Hanno was a sensitive child, much more like his mother than his father. He played the piano by the hour and was afraid of most things and had not the slightest instinct for business. Thomas looked at Hanno sometimes with a quiet desperation. The firm needed a successor. The Buddenbrook name needed to go on.
'Hanno,' he said once, 'you must study. You must learn. The firm will be yours one day.'
Hanno looked at him with Gerda's blue-grey eyes.
'Yes, Papa,' he said quietly.
But Thomas could see that the boy was elsewhere—already elsewhere—in some inner world of music and feeling that had nothing to do with grain prices and shipping contracts.

Thomas was forty-three years old and felt sixty. He ate too much and slept too little. His teeth were giving him trouble. He had them extracted, and after the extraction he caught a chill walking home in the rain, and fell in the street, and was carried home.
He died a few days later, not of anything dramatic—just of the accumulated weight of his life, the long expenditure of himself that had been the firm's prosperity.
The family came and stood around his bed, and Tony wept, and Gerda sat very still with her violin hands in her lap.
The firm went on. The Buddenbrook name went on. For a little while longer.

title: Part IV: Hanno
paragraphs: Johann Buddenbrook the younger—Hanno, as everyone called him—was the last of the Buddenbrooks. He knew it. He felt it in his bones, in the peculiar lassitude that came over him when he thought about the future, in the way the firm's name felt in his mouth like something that belonged to someone else.

He was fifteen when his father died. He was a delicate boy, pale, with his mother's fine features and his mother's passion for music. He attended the gymnasium and was a poor student—not stupid, but absent. His mind was always somewhere else.

The music. When he sat down at the piano, he became someone different—someone real. His compositions were strange and intense for a boy his age, full of harmonic experiments and emotional extremes. His music teacher shook his head and said the boy was gifted but odd.

He had one friend at school—a boy named Kai, the son of a ruined count, who wanted to be a writer. They were alike—both outsiders, both more alive in their imaginations than in the world. They walked together after school and talked about art and books and the future.
'What will you do?' Kai asked once.
'I don't know,' Hanno said. 'What the firm wants, I suppose.'
'But what do you want?'
Hanno was silent for a while.
'I want to make music,' he said. 'Only that.'
'Then do that,' said Kai.
'You don't understand,' said Hanno. 'I am a Buddenbrook.'
Kai looked at him with the sharp, sympathetic eyes of a boy who has understood something that most adults have not.
'The Buddenbrooks are nearly finished,' he said gently. 'Perhaps that means you are free.'

Hanno said nothing. He did not feel free. He felt the weight of the whole family history on his shoulders—the old grandfather who had founded the firm, the consul who had expanded it, the senator who had polished it to a brilliant finish. And now there was only him, and he was not capable of any of it.
He got typhoid fever in the spring, when he was sixteen. It was not a severe case, the doctor said. He should recover.
But Hanno did not want to recover.

This is difficult to say and difficult to understand. He was not unhappy, exactly. He was not suicidal. He simply did not have the will to push back against the fever, the way a person does who wants to live very badly. He lay in his bed and listened to the music in his head—long, complex improvisations that he had never written down and now never would—and the fever rose and fell, and after two weeks he died.
He was sixteen years old.
Tony Buddenbrook was the last one left. She outlived them all—her parents, her brothers, her nephew Hanno. She lived on in a smaller house now, with her daughter Erika and Erika's daughter Elizabeth.

She was very old. She sat by the window sometimes and looked out at the street, and she remembered the great house in Mengstrasse and her grandfather asking her questions from the catechism, and the sunbeam on the floor that was now and then bright and now and then dim.
'What does it mean?' her grandfather had asked.
'It means that it is really and truly true,' she had said, and he had laughed.

She was old and alone and the firm was gone. But when she thought of that moment—the laughter, the sunbeam, the catechism—she felt something she could not name. Something that was neither grief nor gladness but both at once.
She did not know what it meant. She had never known.
Perhaps that too was most certainly true.
